# Git & GitHub

## What Are Git and GitHub?

**Git** is version control software that tracks changes to files over time. Every time you save a snapshot of your work (called a *commit*), Git records what changed, when, and who made the change. This means you can always go back to an earlier version of your code, see exactly what changed between two versions, and understand the full history of an analysis.

**GitHub** is a website that hosts Git repositories in the cloud. Where Git runs on your computer, GitHub is the remote backup and collaboration hub. When you *push* your work to GitHub, it is saved online and accessible to your teammates.

Think of it this way: Git is the system that tracks your changes locally, and GitHub is where those changes get backed up and shared.

## Why We Use It

For CWER projects, Git and GitHub serve three purposes:

1. **Backup.** Code and analysis scripts are backed up to GitHub automatically every time you push. You will never lose work to a crashed hard drive or an accidentally deleted file.
2. **History.** Every commit is a record of what changed and why. When you return to a project after six months, or when a new team member inherits your work, the commit history explains the evolution of the analysis.
3. **Collaboration.** Multiple people can work on the same project without overwriting each other's work. GitHub keeps everything organized under the cwer-yale organization so that lab projects belong to the lab, not to any individual's personal account.

## The cwer-yale Organization

All CWER analysis projects live under the [cwer-yale](https://github.com/cwer-yale) GitHub organization, not under personal accounts. This ensures continuity — if a team member leaves, the project stays accessible to the lab. When you start a new analysis project, create the repository in cwer-yale, not in your personal GitHub account.

To get access to cwer-yale, contact Mike or Joanna with your GitHub username and they will add you.

## Getting Set Up

There are two ways to interact with Git and GitHub: the **terminal** and **GitHub Desktop**. This guide covers the terminal approach, which is more flexible and what you will see referenced throughout this guide. However, GitHub Desktop is a visual application that lets you manage commits, pushes, and pulls through a point-and-click interface rather than typed commands — some people find it easier when starting out.

You can download GitHub Desktop at [desktop.github.com](https://desktop.github.com). Both approaches work fine for CWER projects, and you can switch between them freely — they interact with the same underlying Git repository. If you are comfortable in the terminal, stick with that. If the command line feels intimidating at first, GitHub Desktop is a perfectly reasonable starting point.

### 1. Create a GitHub Account

Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and create a free account. Use a professional username — this account will be visible to collaborators and potentially to future employers. Your Yale email is a reasonable choice for the account email but not manadatory.

### 2. Install Git

Git may be pre-installed. Check whether you have it by running this in the Positron Terminal:
```bash
git --version
```

If you see a version number, you are good. If not, install it by running:
```bash
xcode-select --install
```

### 3. Configure Git with Your Identity

Git records your name and email with every commit. Set these once and they apply to all your projects:
```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@yale.edu"
```

Use the same email address associated with your GitHub account.

### 4. Install the GitHub CLI

The GitHub CLI (`gh`) is the easiest way to authenticate with GitHub and create repositories from the terminal. Install it with Homebrew:
```bash
brew install gh
```

If you do not have Homebrew installed, first run:
```bash
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
```

Then authenticate with GitHub:
```bash
gh auth login
```

Follow the prompts — choose GitHub.com, HTTPS, and authenticate via browser. You will only need to do this once.

### 5. Request Access to cwer-yale

Once your GitHub account is set up, contact Mike or Joanna with your GitHub username to be added to the cwer-yale organization.

## Your Daily Workflow

Once a project is set up, your day-to-day interaction with Git follows a simple cycle: edit your files, stage the changes you want to save, commit them with a message, and push to GitHub.

### Cloning a Repository

When you need to work on an existing project for the first time, *clone* it to your computer. This downloads the full repository, including all files and history.

**Terminal:**
```bash
cd ~/Documents/GitHub
gh repo clone cwer-yale/project-name
```

**GitHub Desktop:**

Click **File → Clone Repository**, select the cwer-yale organization, choose the repository, and select where to save it on your computer.

### The Edit → Stage → Commit → Push Cycle

**Terminal:**

1. Edit your files as you normally would in Positron.

2. Check what has changed:
```bash
git status
```

3. Stage the changes you want to include in this commit:
```bash
git add filename.qmd
```

Or to stage all changed files at once:
```bash
git add .
```

4. Commit with a descriptive message:
```bash
git commit -m "Add descriptive statistics for Wave 1 data"
```

5. Push to GitHub:
```bash
git push
```

**GitHub Desktop:**

1. Edit your files as you normally would in Positron.
2. Open GitHub Desktop — changed files will appear automatically in the left panel.
3. Check the boxes next to the files you want to include in this commit.
4. Write a commit message in the box at the bottom left.
5. Click **Commit to main**.
6. Click **Push origin** in the top bar to send your changes to GitHub.

### Pulling Changes from Teammates

If a teammate has pushed changes to the repository, you need to *pull* them before you start working.

**Terminal:**
```bash
git pull
```

**GitHub Desktop:**

Click **Fetch origin** in the top bar, then **Pull origin** if changes are available.

Make this a habit — pull at the start of every work session to make sure you have the latest version of the project.

## Writing Good Commit Messages

A commit message should complete the sentence: *"This commit will..."*

**Good messages:**
- `Add multilevel model for teacher outcome`
- `Fix merge error in Wave 2 cleaning script`
- `Update descriptives table with revised N`

**Poor messages:**
- `updates`
- `fixed stuff`
- `wip`
- `asdfgh`

Good commit messages make the project history readable. When you or a teammate needs to understand what changed three months ago, a clear commit history saves significant time.

## What Belongs in .gitignore

Not everything should be committed to GitHub. A `.gitignore` file tells Git which files to ignore. For CWER projects, this typically includes:

- `data/raw/` — raw data files, especially anything with participant information, should not be committed to GitHub. Store these in Yale Secure Box or Storage @ Yale instead.
- `data/cleaned/` — cleaned data files can often be regenerated from scripts and may contain sensitive information
- `output/` — generated outputs can be regenerated from code
- `renv/library/` — R package libraries are large and can be restored from `renv.lock`
- `.DS_Store` — Mac system files

A standard `.gitignore` for CWER projects:
```
# Data
data/raw/
data/cleaned/

# Generated outputs
output/

# R
renv/library/
.Rhistory
.RData

# Quarto
_book/
.quarto/
*_files/

# Mac
.DS_Store
```

Note that `renv.lock` **should** be committed — it records the exact package versions for the project and is essential for reproducibility.

## When to Commit

Commit after each meaningful unit of work — not after every single line change, and not just at the end of the day. Good triggers for a commit include:

- Finishing a data cleaning step
- Getting an analysis to run correctly
- Adding a new table or figure
- Fixing a bug
- Completing a section of a script

If you have been working for a few hours and have not committed, that is probably a sign you should commit now.

## CWER Conventions

- All analysis project repositories live in **cwer-yale**, not personal accounts
- Include a `README.md` in every repository with a brief description of the project
- Follow the project folder structure described in the [Project Organization](../data/project-organization.qmd) chapter — `data/`, `code/`, `output/`, `docs/`
- Do not commit raw data with participant information to GitHub — keep sensitive data in Yale Secure Box or Storage @ Yale
- Note AI contributions in commit messages when relevant (e.g., `Add MLM script (developed with Claude assistance)`)
